narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background and academic journey have been carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts performance on the bar exam to ensure we select students likely to succeed on their first try. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.30 sits notably below the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 28 is well under the usual 36.70, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 2 and 1 respectively rank in the lower ranges compared to averages around 5-6—while your middle family income bracket aligns more closely with the norm, and your full-time student status matches what most applicants choose. The model estimates a 96% probability that you would fail the bar exam on the first attempt, a threshold that led to the rejection of your application to protect both your future and our program's outcomes. This high failure risk stems primarily from three key factors in your profile, ranked by their influence on the prediction. Most significantly, your low Year 3 decile ranking strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, as it signals weaker performance relative to peers in later undergrad years. Next, your Year 1 decile also contributed substantially in the same direction, highlighting early academic struggles that the model sees as persistent risks. Your LSAT score rounded out the top influences, further nudging the prediction toward failure by falling short of what's typically needed for strong bar preparation. These elements together outweighed any neutral or positive aspects, like your family background, painting a picture riskier than for most applicants who pass the bar. While your full-time commitment is common and unproblematic, it couldn't offset the academic red flags that set you apart from the stronger profiles the model favors. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, strengthening your grades in any remaining coursework to improve your decile rankings, and perhaps seeking targeted tutoring or bar prep resources tailored for Hispanic students navigating similar paths. Many have turned rejections into successes by addressing these exact areas—your determination as a male from a middle-income Hispanic family positions you well to do the same with focused effort. Reapplying after these improvements could shift the model's view dramatically in your favor.
